summary:
The Roman conquest of Dacia (101–106 AD) transformed the territory of modern Romania into a Roman province, bringing Roman language, culture, law, and urbanization to a region that would later become the only Romance-speaking country in eastern Europe — a testament to the depth of Roman colonization. The conquest occurred in two campaigns by Emperor Trajan against the Dacian king Decebalus: the first (101–102 AD) ended with Dacia as a client state; the second (105–106 AD) destroyed the Dacian kingdom, killed or enslaved much of its population, and established the Roman province of Dacia.

The conquest's significance extended well beyond territorial gain. The Dacians were known for their extraordinary wealth in gold and silver — Trajan's loot reportedly included 165 tons of gold and 330 tons of silver, an enormous windfall that funded the construction of [[event:2021|Trajan's Column]] and the Forum of Trajan in Rome. The province attracted Roman colonists from across the empire (particularly mining specialists), creating a multilingual colonial society. When the Romans abandoned Dacia under Emperor Aurelian in 271–275 AD (too expensive to defend against Gothic and Carpathian pressure), a Romanized population remained — the ancestors of modern Romanians, who call their country 'Romania' (Land of the Romans) and speak a Latin-derived language despite over a millennium of separation from the Roman world.
